title: PostgreSQL
description: Move your app from SQLite to a production-grade PostgreSQL database with Alembic migrations.

steps.title: SQLite vs PostgreSQL
steps.instruction:
## When to switch databases

SQLite is a file-based database - the entire database lives in a single `.db` file on disk. It is excellent for development and learning because it requires no setup. But it has limits:

- **Concurrent writes** - only one process can write at a time. Under real traffic with multiple web workers, writes queue up or fail.
- **No server** - SQLite cannot be on a separate machine from your app. PostgreSQL can.
- **Missing features** - PostgreSQL has full-text search, JSON columns, better indexing, and more.

The rule of thumb: use SQLite while building and learning; switch to PostgreSQL before anyone else uses your app.

### The good news

Because you are using SQLAlchemy, almost none of your application code changes. SQLAlchemy handles the translation between your Python code and the database. The only thing that changes is the **connection string** and the **driver**.

### DATABASE_URL

The standard way to configure a database connection is a single `DATABASE_URL` environment variable:

```
# SQLite
DATABASE_URL=sqlite:///./todos.db

# PostgreSQL
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://username:password@localhost/todos
```

The format is: `driver://user:password@host/database`

Your app reads this from the environment and passes it to `create_engine`. Switching databases is then just a config change.

---

**Your turn:** Set up your app to read `DATABASE_URL` from the environment.
steps.starter_code:
import os
from fastapi import FastAPI
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, Integer, String, Boolean
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, sessionmaker
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

# Read DATABASE_URL from environment, defaulting to SQLite for local dev
DATABASE_URL = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL', 'sqlite:///./todos.db')

engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL)
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=engine)

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class Todo(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'todos'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    text = Column(String, nullable=False)
    done = Column(Boolean, default=False)

Base.metadata.create_all(bind=engine)

app = FastAPI(title='Todo App')
steps.solution_code:
import os
from fastapi import FastAPI
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, Integer, String, Boolean
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, sessionmaker
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

DATABASE_URL = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL', 'sqlite:///./todos.db')

engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL)
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=engine)

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class Todo(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'todos'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    text = Column(String, nullable=False)
    done = Column(Boolean, default=False)

Base.metadata.create_all(bind=engine)

app = FastAPI(title='Todo App')
steps.hint: The starter code is complete - read through it and press Check to continue.

steps.title: Connecting to PostgreSQL
steps.instruction:
## Installing the PostgreSQL driver

SQLAlchemy needs a **driver** to talk to PostgreSQL - a Python package that knows the PostgreSQL wire protocol. The most common one is `psycopg2`:

```bash
pip install psycopg2-binary
```

The `-binary` variant bundles the native library, so you do not need PostgreSQL headers installed on your machine. Use it for development. In production you might use `psycopg2` (without `-binary`) for better performance, but the binary version is fine to start.

### The connection string

```
postgresql://username:password@localhost/todos
```

Breaking it down:
- `postgresql://` - tells SQLAlchemy to use the psycopg2 driver
- `username:password` - your PostgreSQL credentials
- `localhost` - the server (use an IP or hostname in production)
- `/todos` - the database name (create this in PostgreSQL first)

### One SQLite-only setting to remove

If your current `create_engine` call has `connect_args={"check_same_thread": False}`, remove it. That argument is SQLite-only and will cause an error with PostgreSQL.

```python
# SQLite needed this
engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL, connect_args={"check_same_thread": False})

# PostgreSQL does not - remove connect_args entirely
engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL)
```

### Setting DATABASE_URL locally

In your `.env` file:
```
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:yourpassword@localhost/todos
```

Create the `todos` database in PostgreSQL first:
```bash
psql -U postgres -c "CREATE DATABASE todos;"
```

---

**Your turn:** Update `create_engine` so it works with PostgreSQL (no `connect_args`).
steps.starter_code:
import os
from fastapi import FastAPI
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, Integer, String, Boolean
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, sessionmaker
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

DATABASE_URL = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL', 'sqlite:///./todos.db')

# Remove connect_args - it's SQLite-only and will break PostgreSQL
engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL, connect_args={"check_same_thread": False})
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=engine)

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class Todo(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'todos'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    text = Column(String, nullable=False)
    done = Column(Boolean, default=False)

app = FastAPI(title='Todo App')
steps.solution_code:
import os
from fastapi import FastAPI
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, Integer, String, Boolean
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, sessionmaker
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

DATABASE_URL = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL', 'sqlite:///./todos.db')

engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL)
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=engine)

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class Todo(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'todos'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    text = Column(String, nullable=False)
    done = Column(Boolean, default=False)

app = FastAPI(title='Todo App')
steps.hint: Remove `connect_args={"check_same_thread": False}` from the `create_engine` call. The rest stays the same.

steps.title: Alembic setup
steps.instruction:
## Why you need migrations

Right now your app creates tables with `Base.metadata.create_all()`. This works the first time - it creates every table. But it cannot handle changes. If you add a column to your model and restart the app, nothing happens to the existing table. The column is missing and queries fail.

**Alembic** solves this by tracking every change to your schema as a numbered migration file. Running `alembic upgrade head` applies all pending migrations in order.

### Install and initialise

```bash
pip install alembic
alembic init migrations
```

This creates a `migrations/` folder and an `alembic.ini` config file.

### Configure env.py

Alembic needs to know your database URL and your models. Open `migrations/env.py` and make two changes:

**1. Import your models' Base and read DATABASE_URL:**
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from main import Base  # your app's Base class

load_dotenv()
config.set_main_option('sqlalchemy.url', os.getenv('DATABASE_URL'))
```

**2. Set `target_metadata`** so Alembic can compare your models against the database:
```python
target_metadata = Base.metadata
```

This is the key line. Without it, Alembic cannot detect schema changes.

---

**Your turn:** Update `migrations/env.py` to import your Base and set `target_metadata`.
steps.starter_code:
# migrations/env.py (the relevant section)
import os
from logging.config import fileConfig
from sqlalchemy import engine_from_config, pool
from alembic import context
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# Import your app's Base so Alembic knows about your models
from main import Base

load_dotenv()

# Set the database URL from the environment
config = context.config
config.set_main_option('sqlalchemy.url', os.getenv('DATABASE_URL'))

# Tell Alembic about your models
target_metadata = Base.metadata
steps.solution_code:
# migrations/env.py (the relevant section)
import os
from logging.config import fileConfig
from sqlalchemy import engine_from_config, pool
from alembic import context
from dotenv import load_dotenv

from main import Base

load_dotenv()

config = context.config
config.set_main_option('sqlalchemy.url', os.getenv('DATABASE_URL'))

target_metadata = Base.metadata
steps.hint: Import `Base` from your `main` module and assign `target_metadata = Base.metadata`. Also set the URL with `config.set_main_option('sqlalchemy.url', os.getenv('DATABASE_URL'))`.

steps.title: Your first migration
steps.instruction:
## Generating and running a migration

Now that Alembic knows about your models, it can compare them against the actual database and generate the SQL needed to make them match.

### Generate the migration

```bash
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "initial"
```

- `--autogenerate` - Alembic inspects your models and writes the migration for you
- `-m "initial"` - a short description that appears in the filename

This creates a file in `migrations/versions/` like `abc123_initial.py`. Open it and read the `upgrade()` function - it contains the `CREATE TABLE` SQL that Alembic will run.

Always review generated migrations before running them. Alembic is accurate but not perfect - complex changes sometimes need manual adjustment.

### Apply the migration

```bash
alembic upgrade head
```

`head` means "apply all pending migrations, up to the latest one". Alembic creates a `alembic_version` table in your database to track which migrations have run, so it will never apply the same one twice.

### Check what's been applied

```bash
alembic current    # show which migration the database is at
alembic history    # list all migrations
```

### Removing Base.metadata.create_all()

Once you are using Alembic, remove `Base.metadata.create_all(bind=engine)` from your app. Alembic owns the schema now - having both will cause confusion.

---

**Your turn:** Generate and apply your first migration.
steps.starter_code:
# Generate the initial migration (Alembic reads your models)
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "initial"

# Review the generated file in migrations/versions/
# Then apply it:
alembic upgrade head

# Confirm it worked:
alembic current
steps.solution_code:
# Generate the initial migration (Alembic reads your models)
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "initial"

# Review the generated file in migrations/versions/
# Then apply it:
alembic upgrade head

# Confirm it worked:
alembic current
steps.hint: The commands are already written - read through them and press Check to continue.

steps.title: Evolving the schema
steps.instruction:
## Adding a column to an existing table

This is where Alembic earns its place. Add a `priority` column to the Todo model:

```python
class Todo(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'todos'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    text = Column(String, nullable=False)
    done = Column(Boolean, default=False)
    priority = Column(Integer, default=1)  # new
```

Now generate a new migration:

```bash
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "add priority column"
```

Alembic compares your model (which now has `priority`) against the database (which does not) and generates:

```python
def upgrade() -> None:
    op.add_column('todos', sa.Column('priority', sa.Integer(), nullable=True))

def downgrade() -> None:
    op.drop_column('todos', 'priority')
```

`op.add_column` is Alembic's way of writing `ALTER TABLE todos ADD COLUMN priority INTEGER`. The `downgrade()` function lets you undo the migration if needed:

```bash
alembic downgrade -1   # undo the last migration
```

Apply the migration:
```bash
alembic upgrade head
```

The column now exists in the database. Existing rows get the default value of `1`.

---

**Your turn:** Add the `priority` column to the Todo model.
steps.starter_code:
import os
from fastapi import FastAPI
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, Integer, String, Boolean
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, sessionmaker
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

DATABASE_URL = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL', 'sqlite:///./todos.db')

engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL)
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=engine)

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class Todo(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'todos'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    text = Column(String, nullable=False)
    done = Column(Boolean, default=False)
    # Add priority column here (Integer, default=1)

app = FastAPI(title='Todo App')
steps.solution_code:
import os
from fastapi import FastAPI
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, Integer, String, Boolean
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, sessionmaker
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

DATABASE_URL = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL', 'sqlite:///./todos.db')

engine = create_engine(DATABASE_URL)
SessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=engine)

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class Todo(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'todos'
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, index=True)
    text = Column(String, nullable=False)
    done = Column(Boolean, default=False)
    priority = Column(Integer, default=1)

app = FastAPI(title='Todo App')
steps.hint: Add `priority = Column(Integer, default=1)` to the Todo class. Then run `alembic revision --autogenerate -m "add priority column"` followed by `alembic upgrade head`.
